# Preference-drift benchmark

> The cohort benchmark proves Chief learns a **fixed** preference. But real
> preferences **move** — a topic you cared about last quarter stops mattering; a
> new project starts demanding attention. This eval flips every user's
> preferences mid-stream and asks the harder question: **does Chief track a moving
> target, and does it let go of what it over-learned?**

```bash
uv run chief eval --drift      # writes eval/reports/drift.md
```

## Headline (deterministic — regenerated by the command above)

- Held-out interrupt F1 against the **current** truth: **0.86** (learned) →
  **0.69** (the instant preferences flip) → **0.88** (re-learned). The collapse is
  real — Chief is briefly serving the *old* preference — and ±1 feedback climbs it
  back. **91%** of users recover to within 0.05 of their pre-drift quality.
- Of the **30** users whose dropped topic had been escalated to a hard **pin**,
  **100%** had that pin **removed** — the over-learned interrupt does not outlive
  the preference that created it.

## How it's set up

Same 100-user cohort ([`eval/personas.jsonl`](../../eval/personas.jsonl)), same
production primitives (`core.learner.Learner.record`, `core.scorer.score_and_route`),
run in three acts per persona:

| act | rounds | what happens |
|---|---|---|
| **phase A** | 10 | train on the persona's *original* wants — the cohort converges, and quiet wanted topics escalate to hard **pins** (cohort-v2). Snapshot held-out F1 vs the old wants. |
| **drift** | — | flip the preference: **drop** one wanted topic (preferring one a pin now guards, so a pin must be *un*-learned) and **add** one previously unwanted topic. Snapshot held-out F1 vs the *new* wants, un-adapted. |
| **phase B** | 10 | train on the *new* wants. Snapshot held-out F1 vs the new wants again, and check the obsolete pin was torn down. |

The held-out stream (6 jittered events/topic) is scored against whichever
preference is *currently* true, so the three F1 numbers are directly comparable —
they measure the same events against a moving label.

## The recovery curve

```
before drift (vs old wants)  0.86  ← learned
at drift     (vs new wants)  0.69  ← preferences just flipped, Chief still serving the old ones
after drift  (vs new wants)  0.88  ← re-learned
```

The flip only touches two of a persona's ~12 topics, so F1 dips rather than
floors — an honest, proportionate collapse. What matters is that it **comes back**:
dropped corrections decay the weights (and un-pin) for the topic that stopped
mattering, while `should_interrupt` on the newly-wanted topic lifts it exactly as
in the fixed-preference cohort.

## Un-pinning, measured

The subplot is the point of the whole feature. A pin is a hard, content-blind
override — without a removal path, a topic pinned in phase A would interrupt
**forever**, even after the user stops wanting it. Here, the pinned-then-dropped
topic keeps firing its pin at the start of phase B, the user answers each with
`should_not_interrupt`, and the **first** such correction removes the pin
(`core.learner` → `State.remove_pin`). Across the 30 users this happens to,
**every one** is un-pinned inside phase B.

## By feedback-noise tier

| tier | users | F1 before | F1 at drift | F1 after | recovered |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| clean | 21 | 0.87 | 0.68 | 0.87 | 81% |
| light | 38 | 0.86 | 0.68 | 0.87 | 89% |
| noisy | 29 | 0.87 | 0.69 | 0.89 | 97% |
| erratic | 12 | 0.79 | 0.71 | 0.89 | 100% |

`recovered` is measured against **each user's own** pre-drift F1, so the noisiest
tier (which started lower, 0.79) clears its bar most easily — noise costs
re-learning *latency*, not the *ability* to re-learn, the same shape the fixed
cohort shows. Preferences that move are tracked; pins that go stale are let go.

## Files

- [`eval/drift.py`](../../eval/drift.py) — the drift harness + report
- [`eval/reports/drift.md`](../../eval/reports/drift.md) — last generated report
- [`tests/test_drift.py`](../../tests/test_drift.py) — determinism + the pinned numbers + the un-pin claim
- [`docs/eval/cohort-benchmark.md`](cohort-benchmark.md#pins-can-also-be-removed-v3--lifecycle) — the pin lifecycle it exercises
